title: Hack and Slash
subtitle: Working as Producer on a junior game project, balancing creative ambition with what was actually achievable.

intro.heading: Structure as a design tool
intro.body:
UX is about building things from a user perspective, but in a junior school project, there's rarely time for proper research or user testing. When people lack industry experience, things get chaotic fast.

That's where my Producer role came in: guiding the team and balancing creative ambition with what was actually achievable.

role.heading: Producer and team coordinator
role.body:
As Producer, I focused on translating ambition into structure. I started by building a proper GDD that became the foundation for our Jira backlog, then facilitated daily standups and tracked progress toward milestones.

One early challenge was that programmers were scoping tasks too broadly the core systems weren't in place yet, but work was already getting complex. I broke tasks like player movement and the attack system into phases from Beta to Gold, so the team could see exactly what needed to happen and when.

image.alt: Figjam planning and Jira backlog structure

problem.heading: Coordination challenges during development
problem.body:
As we approached the Gold milestone, pressure increased and some workflows stopped being followed even with documentation in place.

The real issue wasn't the tools or the process. It was that people didn't yet have the habit of working within a shared structure.
bullets: Merge conflicts and scene conflicts caused by multiple people editing the same assets
Unclear ownership of scenes and prefabs during development
Communication gaps between programmers, artists, and designers
Team members focusing on details while core gameplay still needed stabilization

approach.heading: Reinforcing production structure
approach.body:
To address these challenges I reinforced the production workflow and clarified ownership within the team.

I introduced stricter rules for scene and prefab ownership, reinforced the Git workflow, and clarified tasks within Jira so the team could stay aligned and focus on stabilizing the game.

outcome.heading: Supporting the team toward the Gold milestone
outcome.body:
Reinforcing ownership and workflow created more communication around problems and that shifted something. Instead of frustration building up between people, the team started directing energy toward how we worked together rather than at each other.

Improved coordination between disciplines helped stabilize development and ensured the project progressed toward the Gold milestone.

keyLearning.heading: Structure needs to be built early
keyLearning.body:
I should have made the importance of communication clearer from day one instead of letting people work too freely at the start. Structure introduced late has to fight against habits already formed.

And for UX specifically: the same principle applies. If you don't define the process early, everyone builds their own version of it.

cards.title: Define and reinforce
cards.body: Setting up workflows early is only half the work. Consistently reinforcing them throughout development is what makes the structure actually stick.

cards.title: Ownership reduces friction
cards.body: Clarifying who owns which scenes and prefabs significantly reduced technical conflicts and helped the team move faster without stepping on each other.
